title: [cross-file] lichen_l2_enable() rollback path leaves callback registered on link_ctx cleanup failure
description:
Interaction: lichen_l2.c:lichen_l2_enable <-> lora_l2.c:lichen_lora_l2_set_rx_callback

Bug: In lichen_l2_enable() enable path (lines 1058-1088):
1. Line 1058: lichen_lora_l2_set_rx_callback(lora_rx_callback, NULL) is called
2. Line 1059: lichen_lora_l2_start() is called
3. If start() fails (lines 1067-1069), callback is cleared
4. If start() succeeds but link_ctx_initialized_here is true and we need rollback (line 1079), we acquire mutexes and clean up link_ctx

However, between line 1051 (unlock rx_mutex after link_ctx init) and line 1058 (register callback), there's no failure check. If lichen_lora_l2_set_rx_callback() is a no-op due to UNINIT state (lora_l2.c:1012-1015), we proceed to start() which will fail because state is UNINIT.

The actual bug is subtler: if start() succeeds but we crash in the rollback path (lines 1079-1086), the callback remains registered pointing to lora_rx_callback, which will call lichen_l2_input() with link_ctx_initialized=0 (we just cleared it). The input function checks this at line 1664 and drops the packet, so it's safe.

Actually reviewing more carefully - the rollback at lines 1079-1086 only runs if ret != 0 AND link_ctx_initialized_here is true. If ret==0, we return success and everything is fine. So this is a false positive for most paths.

BUT: There's still a window. If start() fails, we clear callback at line 1068. Then we check link_ctx_initialized_here at line 1079 and rollback. But what if start() returned 0 (success) and then crashed before the function returned? The RX thread is running, callback is set, but link_ctx_initialized might be cleared by a concurrent disable on another thread.

Actually this is prevented by needing to hold the iface->lock. Downgrading to P3.
close_reason: Added defensive error checking for callback clearing in lichen_l2_enable() rollback path. The callback clearing failure is now logged with LOG_WRN. Added detailed comment explaining why this is SAFE even if clearing fails: (1) link_ctx_initialized is cleared, so lichen_l2_input() will drop packets; (2) RX thread isn't running if start() failed; (3) future enable() will re-register anyway. This addresses the edge case where state transitions to UNINIT between start() failure and callback clearing.
